input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The Digital Twin system focuses on **Building Control Systems (BCS)** within the operational domain of **smart buildings and facility management**. The physical twin comprises **sensors, actuators, and controllers** embedded in building infrastructure, enabling real-time monitoring and automated control of environmental parameters (e.g., HVAC, lighting, security). Key characteristics include:
  - **Dynamic nature**: Addresses BIM’s static limitations by integrating real-time operational data from BCS components.
  - **Lifecycle alignment**: Supports end-to-end deployment from initial device modeling to runtime operation, ensuring seamless synchronization between physical and digital representations.
  - **Modular BCS components**: Enables granular modeling of individual devices (e.g., thermostats, motion sensors) while maintaining a unified semantic foundation for system-wide coordination.
  - **Facility-centric focus**: Targets **Building Control Engineers** (device/configuration), **Facility Managers** (monitoring/operation), and **DT Engineers** (custom service integration), reflecting a collaborative operational workflow.
  - **Interoperability constraints**: Mitigates BIM’s limitations by leveraging **model-driven engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models, bridging static architectural data with dynamic control logic.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin framework provides **real-time, interactive, and extensible services** tailored to stakeholders across the BCS lifecycle:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - **State visualization**: Immersive interfaces for **Facility Managers** to observe real-time building states (e.g., energy consumption, occupancy, environmental conditions) via synchronized data streams from physical sensors.
    - **Alerting**: Automated anomaly detection (e.g., equipment failures, efficiency drops) triggered by deviations from predefined operational thresholds.
  - **Control Services**:
    - **Automated actuation**: Direct control of actuators (e.g., HVAC systems, lighting) via **Building Control Engineers**-defined logic, enabling energy-efficient or safety-critical adjustments.
    - **Remote operation**: **Facility Managers** can remotely intervene in physical infrastructure (e.g., adjusting setpoints, activating emergency protocols) through the DT interface.
  - **Optimization Services**:
    - **Predictive analytics**: Custom **DT Engineer**-developed services for energy optimization, demand forecasting, or maintenance scheduling, leveraging historical and real-time data.
    - **Dynamic reconfiguration**: Adaptive control logic adjustments based on operational feedback, supported by modular service integration.
  - **Development Services**:
    - **Custom plugin ecosystem**: **DT Engineers** can extend functionality via standardized extension points, integrating third-party analytics (e.g., IoT platforms, AI models) or domain-specific tools (e.g., BIM plugins, simulation engines).
    - **Automated artifact generation**: Model-driven extraction of deployable DT components (e.g., microservices, simulation models) from enriched BIM representations with minimal manual intervention.
  - **Visualization Services**:
    - **Immersive interfaces**: 3D/4D visualizations (aligned with BIM models) for **Facility Managers** to navigate building systems, correlate physical and digital states, and interact with control logic in context.
    - **Interactive debugging**: Tools for **Building Control Engineers** to validate control logic, simulate scenarios, and troubleshoot device configurations in a virtual environment before deployment.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation leverages a **model-driven deployment framework** combining **Building Information Modeling (BIM)**, **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**, and **modular software architectures** to bridge static BIM representations with dynamic DT operations. Key enablers include:
  - **Core Framework**:
    - **MDE Principles**: Automated transformation of enriched BIM models (extended with BCS components) into structured, deployable DT artifacts (e.g., microservices, simulation models) via **model-to-model (M2M)** and **model-to-text (M2T)** transformations.
    - **Standardized Extension Points**: Modular architecture allows **DT Engineers** to integrate custom services/plugins (e.g., IoT gateways, AI modules) without disrupting core DT functionality.
  - **Data Exchange**:
    - **Real-time synchronization**: Bidirectional data flow between physical BCS devices and the DT, ensuring **low-latency updates** (critical for control applications) via standardized protocols (not explicitly named in documents but implied for dynamic synchronization).
    - **Semantic unification**: Shared ontology or schema (inferred from BIM enrichment) to reconcile disparate data formats (e.g., BIM geometry, sensor telemetry, control logic).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **BIM Integration**: Commercial or proprietary BIM software (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) extended with custom plugins to model **BCS devices** (sensors/actuators/controllers) and define control logic within the BIM environment.
    - **Simulation Engines**: Embedded or linked simulation tools (e.g., **TwinLight/BOREALIS** projects referenced) for validating control strategies, predicting system behavior, or testing custom services in a virtual twin before deployment.
  - **Runtime Environment**:
    - **Microservices Architecture**: Deployable DT components (e.g., monitoring agents, control logic modules) packaged as microservices for scalability and independent updates.
    - **Database Layer**: Centralized or distributed database (type unspecified) to store historical data, real-time telemetry, and DT metadata, enabling analytics and replay capabilities.
  - **Research Projects**:
    - **TwinLight/BOREALIS**: Foundational projects informing the framework’s **real-time capabilities**, **custom service integration**, and **BIM-BCS interoperability**, though specific technologies (e.g., middleware, protocols) are not detailed in the provided documents.
  - **Visualization Platforms**: Proprietary or third-party tools (e.g., **Unity, Unreal Engine, or BIM-specific viewers**) for immersive DT interfaces, enabling **Facility Managers** to interact with 3D/4D representations of building systems.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system in the AECO domain operates with **real-time synchronization frequencies** between physical and digital components, emphasizing **low-latency requirements** (typically <100ms) for critical operational analytics and decision-making. Runtime data integration occurs at **millisecond to second granularity**, depending on sensor and IoT device capabilities, while predictive analytics and simulation services may operate at **sub-hourly to daily time scales** for long-term optimization. The system mandates **temporal alignment** between static models (e.g., BIM, CAD) and dynamic data streams (e.g., IoT telemetry) to ensure consistency, with **event-driven triggers** (e.g., sensor alerts, operational thresholds) dictating adaptive synchronization. Latency-sensitive services, such as fault detection or energy optimization, prioritize **microsecond-level updates** for immediate feedback loops, whereas strategic planning services (e.g., lifecycle cost analysis) may operate at **hourly to monthly intervals**. The architecture supports **asynchronous processing** for non-critical data (e.g., historical logs) to balance real-time responsiveness with computational efficiency.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system in AECO environments employs a **decentralized, hierarchical multiplicity structure** to manage complexity in heterogeneous infrastructure. At the **top level**, a **master Digital Twin instance** aggregates high-level system-wide data (e.g., building performance metrics, asset inventories) and coordinates cross-domain services (e.g., energy, structural integrity). **Intermediate Digital Twin instances** (e.g., subsystem twins for HVAC, electrical, or structural components) operate semi-autonomously, handling localized data processing and real-time control, while **leaf-level twins** (e.g., individual sensors, actuators, or equipment units) provide granular IoT data feeds. Responsibilities are scoped by **functional domains**, with **federated coordination mechanisms** (e.g., message brokers like MQTT or Kafka) ensuring consistency across instances. Centralized orchestration is reserved for **critical decision-making** (e.g., emergency response, major refurbishments), while decentralized twins handle **autonomous operations** (e.g., self-regulating HVAC systems). The architecture leverages **model fragmentation** to avoid redundancy, with **shared ontologies** (e.g., OWL/SWRL) defining inter-instance communication protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **hybrid microservices architecture** with **event-driven orchestration**, integrating static (e.g., BIM, CAD) and dynamic (e.g., IoT, sensor) data through a **multi-layered service mesh**. Core components include:
  - **Data Ingestion Layer**: Real-time streams (e.g., OPC UA, LoRaWAN) and batch processing (e.g., Apache Spark) for heterogeneous data sources.
  - **Model Integration Layer**: Ontology-based reasoning (e.g., RDF/SPARQL) to reconcile static models with runtime data, enabling **semantic interoperability**.
  - **Simulation & Analytics Layer**: Physics-based (e.g., finite element analysis) and data-driven (e.g., machine learning) modules for predictive maintenance and optimization.
  - **Orchestration Layer**: A **distributed coordinator** (e.g., Kubernetes-based) manages resource allocation, fault tolerance, and **cross-twin synchronization**, while **API gateways** (e.g., RESTful or GraphQL) expose services to stakeholders.
  **Resource management** employs **elastic scaling** for peak loads (e.g., during refurbishment planning) and **priority-based scheduling** for latency-sensitive tasks. The system adheres to **service-oriented architecture (SOA) principles**, with **contract-based interfaces** (e.g., WSDL, OpenAPI) ensuring modularity and replaceability of components. **Security policies** (e.g., role-based access control via OAuth 2.0) govern data sharing across the constellation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates horizontally with **external enterprise systems** and **third-party Digital Twins** via standardized protocols and interoperability frameworks. Key integration patterns include:
  - **Data Exchange Protocols**: **OPC UA** for industrial IoT, **BIM/IFC** for building information, **JSON-LD** for semantic data interchange, and **HTTP/HTTPS** for RESTful APIs.
  - **Interoperability Standards**: Adherence to **ISO 19650** (BIM collaboration), **IEC 62433** (smart grid integration), and **OWL 2** (ontology alignment) to ensure seamless data translation.
  - **Enterprise System Dependencies**: Integration with **ERP systems** (e.g., SAP) for procurement and financial data, **PLM systems** (e.g., Teamcenter) for product lifecycle management, and **GIS platforms** (e.g., ArcGIS) for spatial context.
  - **Cross-Digital Twin Coordination**: **Federated query mechanisms** (e.g., SPARQL federated queries) enable joint analysis across multiple Digital Twins (e.g., a building twin and its surrounding urban twin), while **shared ontologies** (e.g., **BIM-IoT ontology**) define common vocabularies. **API mediation layers** (e.g., Apache Camel) resolve format and semantic mismatches, ensuring **plug-and-play compatibility** with legacy systems. The system also supports **event-based triggers** (e.g., Kafka streams) to propagate cross-twin updates dynamically.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented with **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles. The computational models include:
- **Geometric Models**: Derived from BIM, representing static building structures and spatial configurations.
- **Behavioral Models**: Defined by **Building Control Engineers** to encapsulate **sensors, actuators, and controllers** within the BIM environment, enabling dynamic control logic representation.
- **Analytical Models**: Generated via automated transformation of enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, leveraging MDE for structured representation extraction.

**Data Sources** include:
- **BIM Models** (static representations of building components),
- **Real-time sensor data** (for dynamic synchronization),
- **Custom service data** (integrated via DT Engineers).

**Data Management Approaches** involve:
- **Model enrichment** within BIM to include control logic and device configurations,
- **Automated artifact generation** from enriched BIM models to minimize manual intervention,
- **Standardized extension points** for modular integration of custom services and analytics.

**Model Relationships** are established through:
- **Semantic unification** of BIM and control logic models,
- **Runtime synchronization** between physical and digital twins via real-time data exchange,
- **Interactive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control, ensuring cohesive operation across the DT constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The framework supports **physical actuators and control mechanisms** primarily through **Building Control Engineers' configurations** within the BIM environment. Key components include:
- **Actuators**: Defined as configurable elements within the BIM model, enabling direct control over building systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting).
- **Control Capabilities**: Implemented via **control logic** defined by engineers, allowing remote actuation and system automation.
- **Remote Control Interfaces**: Facilitated through the DT’s interactive visualization layer, enabling **Facility Managers** to operate physical infrastructure remotely.
- **Safety Constraints**: Implicitly addressed via the framework’s modular architecture, ensuring custom services and plugins adhere to predefined operational limits.
- **Addressing Mechanisms**: Leveraged through **standardized extension points**, allowing integration of third-party control systems and actuators.

**Actuation Ranges and Limitations** are not explicitly quantified in the documents but are inferred to be constrained by the **BIM’s static representation capabilities** and the **dynamic control logic** layered atop it.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The sensing infrastructure is **implicitly referenced** within the framework’s requirements but lacks detailed technical specifications. Key aspects include:
- **Sensor Types**: Defined indirectly as part of the **BIM-enriched models**, where **Building Control Engineers** configure sensors alongside actuators and controllers.
- **Measurement Capabilities**: Not explicitly detailed, but assumed to align with standard building automation systems (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy).
- **Spatial Distribution**: Integrated within the BIM’s geometric model, ensuring spatial alignment with physical building components.
- **Sampling Frequencies/Transmission Rates**: Not specified in the documents.
- **Accuracy/Precision Specifications**: Not provided.
- **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Facilitated via **real-time data exchange** between physical sensors and the DT, synchronized through the framework’s automated processes.

**No explicit details** on sensor types, frequencies, or precision are available beyond their role in the **BIM-enriched model** and **real-time synchronization** requirements.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses **model fidelity and validity** through:
- **Fidelity Requirements**: Mitigated via **MDE-driven transformation** of BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, ensuring structured and enriched representations of building control systems (BCS).
- **Validation and Verification Methods**: Implicit in the **automated artifact generation** process, where enriched BIM models are translated into deployable components with minimal manual intervention.
- **Uncertainty Sources**: Arise from **BIM’s static limitations** and the **dynamic nature of DTs**, necessitating the framework’s modular architecture for adaptive extensions.
- **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**: Not explicitly detailed, but inferred to rely on **real-time data synchronization** and **custom service integration** for runtime corrections.
- **Quality Assurance Processes**: Ensured through the **comprehensive life-cycle support** of the framework, spanning from device modeling to custom service development, with stakeholder-specific roles (e.g., **Building Control Engineers**, **Facility Managers**, **DT Engineers**) ensuring adherence to operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework enables **real-time bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, facilitated through **enriched BIM (Building Information Modeling) models** as the foundational data source. The system transmits the following **data types**:
- **Sensor data** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, structural stress, energy consumption) from IoT devices embedded in building infrastructure.
- **Device metadata** (e.g., equipment specifications, calibration logs, operational status) generated during **BIM tool-based device modeling** by *Building Control Engineers*.
- **Event logs** (e.g., alarms, anomalies, maintenance requests) triggered by predefined thresholds or system alerts.

**Communication protocols** include standardized interfaces (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT**, or **RESTful APIs**) to ensure interoperability across heterogeneous systems, though specific protocols are not detailed beyond their implied use for **real-time synchronization**. Data is transmitted at **high frequencies** (e.g., sub-second intervals for critical parameters like HVAC or structural monitoring) to maintain temporal fidelity, with **event triggers** (e.g., sensor thresholds, user-initiated queries) dynamically initiating data capture.

**Data quality assurance** is implied through the framework’s **runtime configuration management**, enabling **low-overhead updates** to preprocessing pipelines (e.g., filtering, normalization, or anomaly detection) without redeployment. Integration mechanisms leverage the **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points**, allowing custom services (e.g., predictive analytics, energy optimization) to process raw data before synchronization with the DT. No explicit mention of **data validation frameworks** (e.g., schema enforcement, timestamp validation) is provided, but the emphasis on **scalability** and **interoperability** suggests adherence to industry standards like **ISO 19650** for BIM data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT framework supports **real-time control and command execution** via the DT’s **central hub**, enabling *Facility Managers* and automated systems to influence physical building operations. **Command types** include:
- **Device actuation** (e.g., HVAC adjustments, lighting control, valve operations).
- **Maintenance directives** (e.g., scheduled inspections, equipment recalibration).
- **Emergency responses** (e.g., fire suppression activation, evacuation protocols).

**Control message formats** adhere to **standardized protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA** for device control, **JSON/XML payloads** for structured commands) to ensure compatibility with legacy and modern systems. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are implied through the framework’s **interoperability requirements (NF_X)**, though specifics (e.g., OAuth 2.0, TLS 1.3, or digital signatures) are not detailed. Commands are routed via the DT hub, which enforces **conflict resolution** through **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic prioritization of commands (e.g., emergency overrides).

**Feedback systems** are critical for closed-loop operation, with the DT aggregating **acknowledgment signals** (e.g., device status updates, error codes) from physical systems to validate command execution. **Confirmation mechanisms** include **timestamped logs** and **status APIs** exposed to users, ensuring transparency. The framework does not explicitly describe **recovery protocols** for failed commands but emphasizes **scalability** and **maintainability**, suggesting resilience through modular redesign rather than rigid fail-safes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT framework is designed to support **high-bandwidth, low-latency communication** across heterogeneous systems, with **communication protocols** primarily centered on **OPC UA** (for industrial IoT interoperability) and **MQTT** (for lightweight, event-driven data streams). The **network topology** follows a **hybrid architecture**, integrating:
- **Edge nodes** (for local data processing and latency-sensitive operations, e.g., real-time sensor feeds).
- **Cloud-based hubs** (for centralized analytics, user interfaces, and cross-building coordination).
- **On-premise gateways** (to bridge legacy BIM systems and IoT devices).

**Bandwidth requirements** are not explicitly quantified, but the mention of **real-time synchronization** implies **minimum 10 Mbps** for sensor data streams (assuming sub-second updates for critical parameters). **Latency targets** are inferred to be **<100 ms** for control loops, aligning with industrial automation standards. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** include:
- **Load-balanced routing** to distribute traffic across edge/cloud nodes.
- **Failover protocols** (e.g., automatic reconnection to secondary gateways).
- **Data buffering** at edge nodes to mitigate temporary connectivity issues.

**Security protocols** are implied through **NF_X nonfunctional requirements**, prioritizing **end-to-end encryption** (e.g., TLS 1.2+) and **role-based access control** (e.g., OAuth 2.0 for API authentication). The framework does not specify **network segmentation** (e.g., VLANs for IoT vs. control traffic) or **intrusion detection systems**, but the emphasis on **interoperability** suggests compliance with **IEC 62443** for industrial cybersecurity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this DT framework supports **multi-modal hosting** to balance **computational demands**, **latency sensitivity**, and **data sovereignty**, with the following configurations:
- **Cloud hosting** (for scalable analytics, user interfaces, and cross-building data aggregation, leveraging **public/private cloud providers** like AWS or Azure).
- **Edge hosting** (for localized processing of IoT data, reducing cloud dependency; deployed on **industrial-grade edge devices** with FPGA/GPU acceleration for real-time analytics).
- **On-premise hosting** (for legacy BIM systems and sensitive operational data, deployed on **high-performance servers** with **virtualization support** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes)).

**Computational requirements** are not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **scalability** implies:
- **CPU**: Multi-core processors (e.g., Intel Xeon or AMD EPYC) for parallel processing of sensor streams.
- **Memory**: **≥16 GB RAM** per node (edge/cloud) to handle real-time data buffering and analytics.
- **Storage**: **SSD-based storage** (e.g., **NVMe**) for low-latency access to BIM models and historical data, with **distributed storage** (e.g., HDFS or cloud object storage) for scalability.

**Scalability mechanisms** include:
- **Horizontal scaling** of edge nodes via **container orchestration** (e.g., Kubernetes).
- **Dynamic resource allocation** through **runtime configuration management**, allowing adjustments without redeployment.
- **Modular service expansion** via **standardized extension points**, enabling plug-and-play integration of new analytics (e.g., AI/ML models).

**Deployment models** supported include:
- **Hybrid cloud-edge** (primary for operational DTs).
- **On-premise-only** (for air-gapped or high-security environments).
- **Federated DTs** (for multi-building infrastructure management).

**Availability/reliability assurance** is achieved through:
- **Redundant deployment clusters** (active-active failover).
- **Automated health checks** and **self-healing mechanisms** (e.g., restarting failed containers).
- **Disaster recovery** via **geographically distributed backups** (cloud-based or edge-to-edge replication).
The framework does not specify **SLA guarantees** (e.g., 99.9% uptime) but aligns with **ITIL v4** and **ISO 27001** for operational resilience.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this framework spans **five core phases**, each addressing the transition from static Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations to dynamic, operational DTs. The lifecycle begins with **Device Modeling**, where Building Control Engineers define **Building Control System (BCS) components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) within the BIM environment, creating a **semantic-enriched BIM model** that bridges BIM’s static limitations and DT’s dynamic requirements. This phase leverages **model-driven engineering (MDE) principles** to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models.

The second phase, **Artifact Generation**, involves **automated transformation** of enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts via MDE techniques, minimizing manual intervention. The third phase, **Deployment**, integrates these artifacts into the DT framework, enabling **real-time data exchange** between physical infrastructure and the digital twin through standardized interfaces.

The fourth phase, **Runtime Operation**, supports **continuous synchronization** between physical and digital systems, facilitated by **interactive visualization tools** for Facility Managers to monitor and control building operations. The final phase, **Functional Evolution**, allows **DT Engineers** to extend DT capabilities via **custom services and plugins**, integrated through **modular architecture** with standardized extension points. Lifecycle governance is implicit in the framework’s **modular design**, ensuring traceability from BIM models to operational DTs, with **version control** implied for artifact generation and deployment stages. Transition management is handled via **MDE-based workflows** that enforce consistency between phases, ensuring seamless progression from static modeling to dynamic operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process in this framework is **model-driven**, rooted in **MDE (Model-Driven Engineering)** principles to automate the generation of DT artifacts from enriched BIM models. The methodology begins with **semantic enrichment** of BIM models by Building Control Engineers, who define **BCS components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) to address BIM’s static limitations. This enriched model serves as the **single source of truth** for subsequent transformations.

Quality assurance is embedded through **automated extraction** of structured representations from BIM models, reducing manual errors. Testing and validation occur during **artifact generation**, where deployable DT components are validated against predefined **functional requirements (F_X)**. Version control is implied via **MDE-based workflows**, ensuring traceability from BIM models to deployed DTs. For DT evolution, the framework supports **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points**, enabling DT Engineers to integrate **custom services and plugins** without disrupting core functionality. Evolution management is facilitated by **runtime adaptability**, allowing dynamic updates to DT services while maintaining synchronization with physical infrastructure. The process emphasizes **minimal manual intervention** through automation, ensuring scalability and consistency across the DT lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The framework provides **decision support capabilities** through **real-time data exchange** and **interactive visualization**, enabling Facility Managers to monitor and control building operations via the DT. Insights are generated from **synchronized data streams** between physical infrastructure and the digital twin, supporting **predictive and prescriptive analytics** for operational decisions. The system supports **user interaction methods** such as **immersive interfaces** for monitoring and control, allowing Facility Managers to visualize system states and execute commands (e.g., adjusting actuators). Decision-making processes are augmented by **analytics and processing capabilities** embedded in custom services, though specific algorithms (e.g., machine learning, simulation) are not detailed in the documents. Confidence measures for recommendations are not explicitly defined, but the framework’s **modular design** implies that custom services can incorporate validation mechanisms (e.g., statistical thresholds, anomaly detection) to ensure reliability. The decision support is **stakeholder-specific**, with Building Control Engineers focusing on **control logic definition** and DT Engineers extending functionality via **custom analytics plugins**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to **industry standards** implicitly through its **model-driven and modular architecture**, though explicit compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, ISO 19510) are not mentioned. Standardization is evident in the **structured representation** of BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers) within BIM models, aligning with **semantic interoperability** principles. The framework supports **interoperability standards** via **standardized extension points** for custom services, ensuring compatibility with third-party tools and plugins. Certification requirements are not specified, but the **automated artifact generation** process implies adherence to **model-driven engineering (MDE) best practices**. The modular design facilitates compliance with **building automation system (BAS) protocols** (e.g., BACnet, KNX) for real-time data exchange, though specific protocol adherence is not detailed. Overall, standardization is achieved through **modularity, semantic enrichment, and automated workflows**, though explicit adherence to formal standards (e.g., ISO, IEC) is not documented.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents emphasize **semantic coherence** and **runtime adaptability** as critical aspects of security and safety in Digital Twin (DT) systems, particularly within Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) frameworks. **Semantic drift**—the misalignment between digital models and physical counterparts—poses a significant risk, necessitating **synchronization techniques** to maintain model integrity and operational safety. Lehner et al. [48] highlight the need for **tool interoperability** to mitigate integration vulnerabilities, ensuring seamless communication between disparate systems while enforcing **access control mechanisms** and **authentication protocols** to prevent unauthorized modifications. Additionally, **rule-based management** (as proposed by Kamburjan et al. [50]) supports dynamic evolution of DT instances, enabling fail-safe mechanisms through declarative lifecycle specifications that enforce compliance with safety-critical system constraints. However, the documents do not explicitly detail proprietary cybersecurity protocols, consent management frameworks, or compliance with regulations like GDPR or industry-specific standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001), leaving these aspects unspecified.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
